glossary.def: Sliding a small learnable kernel across an input and recording, at each position, the sum of element-wise products with the patch it overlaps. Deep-learning libraries skip the kernel flip of mathematical convolution and use cross-correlation; because the kernel is learned, the distinction does not matter. It encodes two priors: local connectivity (each output sees only a small window) and weight sharing (the same weights are reused everywhere).

The small tensor of weights a convolution slides over its input, typically 3x3 in modern networks. A kernel applied to a C-channel input is k x k x C and produces one output feature map; a layer runs many kernels in parallel, one per output channel. Its weights are the only parameters of the convolution and are reused at every spatial position.

The 2D output a single kernel produces by being applied across the whole input: a spatial record of where that kernel's pattern occurs. A convolutional layer outputs a stack of feature maps, one per output channel; early-layer maps respond to edges and textures, deep-layer maps to object parts.

Convolution is equivariant: shift the input and the feature map shifts identically, because the same shared weights run at every location. Pooling converts a little equivariance into local invariance: shift the input slightly and the pooled output does not change, because the summary (e.g. the max) ignores where in the window the activation occurred. Equivariance preserves location; invariance discards it.

A parameter-free operation that slides a window over a feature map and replaces each region with a single summary value, usually the maximum (max-pooling) or the mean. A 2x2 stride-2 pool halves each spatial dimension (quartering the area) and grants small-shift invariance. Modern networks often replace it with strided convolutions, but global average pooling remains the standard bridge from the final feature map to a linear classifier.

The region of the original input that influences a single unit in a deep feature map. It grows with depth by the recursion r_l = r_{l-1} + (k-1) * j_{l-1}, where the jump j (feature stride) multiplies by the stride at each layer; stacking 3x3 convs grows it linearly, while inserting a stride-2 pool doubles the jump and makes every later layer reach twice as far. The effective receptive field is usually smaller than this theoretical one.

Adding a block's input back to its output, y = F(x) + x, so the layers learn only a residual correction rather than the full mapping. Introduced by ResNet (2015), it lets unneeded layers default to the identity and gives gradients a direct path to early layers, defeating the degradation that stopped plain networks past ~20 layers. The residual stream it created underpins essentially every deep architecture today, Transformers included.

Reusing a CNN pre-trained on a large dataset (typically ImageNet) instead of training from scratch. In feature-extraction mode the backbone is frozen and only a new classifier head is trained; in fine-tuning mode some or all of the backbone is unfrozen and adapted at a small learning rate. It works because early-layer features are near-universal and transferability falls with depth, so freezing the bottom and retraining the top reaches strong accuracy from hundreds rather than millions of examples.

refs.cite: LeCun, Y., Bottou, L., Bengio, Y. & Haffner, P. (1998). Gradient-Based Learning Applied to Document Recognition. Proceedings of the IEEE 86(11).
Krizhevsky, A., Sutskever, I. & Hinton, G. E. (2012). ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks. NeurIPS 25.
Simonyan, K. & Zisserman, A. (2015). Very Deep Convolutional Networks for Large-Scale Image Recognition. ICLR 2015.
Szegedy, C. et al. (2015). Going Deeper with Convolutions (GoogLeNet/Inception). CVPR 2015.
He, K., Zhang, X., Ren, S. & Sun, J. (2016). Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition (ResNet). CVPR 2016.
Yosinski, J., Clune, J., Bengio, Y. & Lipson, H. (2014). How Transferable Are Features in Deep Neural Networks? NeurIPS 27.
Dosovitskiy, A. et al. (2021). An Image Is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale (ViT). ICLR 2021.
